<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[en:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[ja:スクモ塚古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11311482]] Wikidata name: Sukumozuka Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sukumozuka Kofun | native_name =スクモ塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Sukumoduka(Masuda-city).jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.701136|N|131.848392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Masuda|WD=Q753731}} | region = [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.late 45th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|42|4.09|N|131|50|54.21|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[en:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[ja:スクモ塚古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11311482]] Wikidata name: Sukumozuka Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sukumozuka Kofun | native_name =スクモ塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Sukumoduka(Masuda-city).jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.701136|N|131.848392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Masuda|WD=Q753731}} | region = [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.late 45th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|42|4.09|N|131|50|54.21|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[en:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[ja:スクモ塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[en:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[ja:スクモ塚古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[en:Sukumozuka Kofun]][[ja:スクモ塚古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11311482]] Wikidata name: Sukumozuka Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] | name =Sukumozuka Kofun | native_name =スクモ塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Sukumoduka(Masuda-city).jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.701136|N|131.848392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Masuda|WD=Q753731}} | region = [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.late 45th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|42|4.09|N|131|50|54.21|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped Kofun]], located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Masuda, Shimane|Masuda, Shimane]] in the [[:en:San'in region|San'in region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the [[:en:Iwami Province|Iwami region]] and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent [[:en:Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|Ministry of Education]] commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and [[:en:Toro (archaeological site)|Toro Ruins]]. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''[[:en:Zenpokoenfun|zenpō-kōen-fun]]''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Masuda Station|Masuda Station]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>

For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|WD=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education}} commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro Ruins}}. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun}}, located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Masuda, Shimane|en|Masuda, Shimane|WD=Q753731|lt=Masuda, Shimane}} in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the {{ill|Iwami Province|en|Iwami Province|WD=Q1053709|lt=Iwami region}} and the largest in Shimane prefecture.

There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|WD=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education}} commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro Ruins}}. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons category-inline|Sukumozuka Kofun}} *[https://masudashi.com/kankouspot/kankouspot-1295/ Masuda City Tourist Association home page]{{in lang|ja}}

The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun}}, located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Masuda, Shimane|en|Masuda, Shimane|WD=Q753731|lt=Masuda, Shimane}} in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the {{ill|Iwami Province|en|Iwami Province|WD=Q1053709|lt=Iwami region}} and the largest in Shimane prefecture.

The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|WD=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}} commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro Ruins|1=Toro (archaeological site)}}. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station|1=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>

It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station|1=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

==External links== {{commons category-inline|Sukumozuka Kofun}} *[https://masudashi.com/kankouspot/kankouspot-1295/ Masuda City Tourist Association home page]{{in lang|ja}}

The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the {{ill|Iwami Province|en|Iwami Province|WD=Q1053709|lt=Iwami region|1=Iwami Province}} and the largest in Shimane prefecture.

For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|WD=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}} commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro Ruins|1=Toro (archaeological site)}}. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpōkōenfun|1=[[Zenpōkōenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station|1=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shimane)]]

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} | name =Sukumozuka Kofun | native_name =スクモ塚古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Sukumoduka(Masuda-city).jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_type = Japan Shimane Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Sukumozuka Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.701136|N|131.848392|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Masuda|WD=Q753731|1=Masuda, Shimane}} | region = {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}} | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.late 45th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|42|4.09|N|131|50|54.21|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|'''Sukumozuka Kofun'''|スクモ塚古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=keyhole-shaped Kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}}, located in the Kugi neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Masuda, Shimane|en|Masuda, Shimane|WD=Q753731|lt=Masuda, Shimane|1=Masuda, Shimane}} in the {{ill|San'in region|en|San'in region|WD=Q398310|lt=San'in region|1=San'in region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumulus was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1941.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2205|title= スクモ塚古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2021}}</ref> It is the largest in the {{ill|Iwami Province|en|Iwami Province|WD=Q1053709|lt=Iwami region|1=Iwami Province}} and the largest in Shimane prefecture.
==Overview== The Sukumozuka Kofun is located in the Kushiro Hills at an elevation of about 40 meters near the Masuda River. For more than a dozen centuries after its construction, it was overgrown with vegetation and looked like a thicket of trees. It was discovered in 1939 by local historian Yatomi Kumaichirō and from 1941, it was [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] and surveyed by Ueda Sanpei, a prominent {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|WD=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}} commissioner who excavated the [[Heijō Palace]] and {{ill|Toro (archaeological site)|en|Toro (archaeological site)|WD=Q673607|lt=Toro Ruins|1=Toro (archaeological site)}}. It was designated as a National Historic Site on December 13 of the same year as a large {{ill|Round Kofun|lt=circular|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} {{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} Kofun; however, the designation was immediately controversial as many archaeologists argued that it was a {{nihongo|''{{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}}''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=[[Zenpō-kōen-fun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}}, which is shaped like a keyhole, having one square end and one circular end, when viewed from above, or else a {{ill|''{{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because of the mass replacement error that gets rid of the links like this otherwise|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shaped like two co-joined rectangles.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref> The issue was only settled in October 2022, when the Masuda City Board of Education announced that findings confirmed the Sukumozuka Kofun to be a keyhole-shaped Kofun.
The tumulus has a total length of 100 meters, the diameter of the posterior round mound is about 47 meters with a height from its surroundings of seven meters. The surface of the tumulus is covered with ''[[fukiishi]],'' the size of a human head, and had two rows of cylindrical ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}''. There is a 17 x 15 meter square structure on the north side of the circular portion, presumably a ceremonial platform. The anterior portion is 19 meters wide at the neck, 29 meters wide at the end, and 2.5 meters high on the north side.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
There were formerly small circular ''baizuka'' accompanying tumuli on both sides of the main mound. The mounds were ten meters in diameter with a height of 2.6 meters, but the ones on the east side have disappeared, and the ones on the west side have hardly preserved their original shape.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
Judging from the size and style of the ''kofun'', it is believed to have been built from the end of the early to early middle Kofun period (late 4th century), and to be the tomb of the chieftain who ruled the Masuda region. It is located about 15 minutes by bus from the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Masuda Station|en|Masuda Station|WD=Q6785654|lt=Masuda Station|1=Masuda Station}}.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
